items.foco: Prodromal period with cough, coryza and conjunctivitis
items.title: Measles prodrome
items.contentHtml:
Prodromal period with cough, coryza and conjunctivitis

  • The incubation period of measles is 10 to 12 days, followed by a prodrome of 2 to 4 days with cough, coryza and conjunctivitis (catarrhal triple C).
  • High fever (>39 °C) appears in this phase and is maintained during the rash; The cough is dry and persistent, the conjunctivitis is non-purulent with photophobia.
  • This catarrhal condition is key to the differential diagnosis with rubella (mild prodrome, without intense cough) and roseola infantum (high fever without marked catarrhal symptoms).
  • In malnourished or immunocompromised children, the prodrome may be atypical or longer, increasing the risk of complications.

items.title: Koplik spots
items.contentHtml:
Koplik spots on the oral mucosa

  • Koplik spots are pathognomonic of measles: small whitish lesions (like grains of salt) on an erythematous base on the buccal mucosa, opposite the lower molars.
  • They appear 1 to 2 days before the rash and persist up to 2 days later; Their presence confirms the clinical diagnosis even before the skin rash.
  • They should not be confused with Forchheimer's sign (petechial spots on the soft palate, typical of rubella) or with oral thrush.
  • The absence of Koplik's spots does not rule out measles, especially in vaccinated people or in late stages of the prodrome.

items.title: Rash progression
items.contentHtml:
Maculopapular rash with cephalocaudal progression

  • The maculopapular rash begins at the hairline and behind the ears, extending cephalocaudal to the trunk and extremities in 3 to 4 days.
  • The lesions converge to form erythematous plaques, unlike rubella (finer, non-confluent rash) and roseola infantum. (sudden rash after defervescence).
  • Cephalocaudal progression is a classic clinical criterion; The presence of Hutchinson's sign (fine peeling that resolves) is typical of measles.
  • In severe cases, the rash can be hemorrhagic (black measles), associated with higher mortality.

items.title: Fever in measles
items.contentHtml:
High fever during the rash

  • High fever (>39 °C) persists during the exanthematous phase and gradually subsides as the rash disappears (defervescence in 3 to 5 days).
  • If the fever persists beyond 5 days or reappears, suspect complications such as superimposed bacterial pneumonia or encephalitis.
  • In the differential diagnosis, roseola infantum presents high fever that It disappears abruptly when the rash appears (fever without rash, then rash without fever).
  • High fever in measles is due to the systemic inflammatory response mediated by T lymphocytes against the measles virus (paramyxovirus).

items.title: Serious complications
items.contentHtml:
Complication: pneumonia and encephalitis

  • Pneumonia is the most common cause of death from measles, it can be interstitial viral (Warthin-Finkeldey giant cells) or superimposed bacterial (Streptococcus pneumoniae, Staphylococcus aureus).
  • Acute encephalitis occurs in 1 in 1,000. cases, it appears 5 to 7 days after the rash, with lethargy, convulsions and coma; mortality of 15% and neurological sequelae in 25%.
  • Another late complication is subacute sclerosing panencephalitis (SSPE), months or years later, with progressive neurological deterioration.
  • The risk of complications is greater in infants <12 months, malnourished (vitamin A deficiency) and immunocompromised.

items.title: Diagnostic confirmation
items.contentHtml:
Serological diagnosis (IgM) or PCR

  • Laboratory confirmation is performed by detection of specific immunoglobulin M (IgM) against measles virus in serum, positive from the first day of the rash until 4 weeks later.
  • Real-time polymerase chain reaction (PCR) of nasopharyngeal swab or urine is more sensitive in the first 3 days of the rash and allows genotyping.
  • Viral culture is not recommended due to its low sensitivity and slowness.
  • In recent vaccinated people (4 to 30 days), IgM may be positive due to the vaccine; PCR differentiates vaccine strain from wild type.

items.title: Epidemiological notification
items.contentHtml:
Immediate mandatory notification

  • Measles is a disease of immediate mandatory notification to the health authority (suspected + confirmed case) within the first 24 hours.
  • The epidemiological investigation form must be completed with vaccination, contact and travel data.
  • In the event of a suspected case, control measures must be implemented: isolation, active search for contacts and blocking vaccination (MMR within 72 hours post-exposure).
  • Failure to notify can lead to outbreaks and legal sanctions.

items.title: MMR vaccination
items.contentHtml:
MMR vaccine (MMR) at 12 months

  • The MMR vaccine (measles, rubella, mumps) is administered subcutaneously at 12 months of age (first dose) and a booster at 18 months or 4-6 years, depending on the schedule.
  • The effectiveness of one dose is 93% and with two doses it reaches the 97%.
  • Contraindications: pregnancy, severe immunosuppression, allergy to neomycin or gelatin, severe acute febrile infection.
  • In exposed infants <12 months, intravenous immunoglobulin (400 mg/kg) can be used within 6 days post-exposure to prevent or attenuate the disease.

items.foco: Greater risk in infants and malnourished
items.title: Risk groups
items.contentHtml:
Higher risk in infants and malnourished

  • Infants <12 months have a higher risk of severe measles because maternal antibodies decline before the age of vaccination and their immune system is immature.
  • Malnutrition (especially vitamin A deficiency) increases the incidence of complications: pneumonia, severe diarrhea, blindness due to keratoconjunctivitis and death.
  • The rate of Fatality in malnourished children can exceed 10% in endemic areas.
  • In immunocompromised patients (HIV, transplant, chemotherapy), measles can be atypical (without rash) and with high mortality due to giant cell pneumonia.

items.foco: Treatment with vitamin A reduces mortality
items.title: Vitamin A in measles
items.contentHtml:
Treatment with vitamin A reduces mortality

  • Vitamin A reduces mortality and the severity of complications in children with measles, especially in malnourished or vitamin A deficient children.
  • Dose: 200,000 IU (international units) orally once a day for 2 consecutive days in children >12 months; in infants 6-12 months: 100,000 IU; in <6 months: 50,000 IU.
  • The World Health Organization recommends administering vitamin A to all children with measles, regardless of nutritional status, in areas with high mortality.
  • The mechanism includes restoration of epithelial integrity and modulation of the immune response.

items.foco: Respiratory isolation for 4 days of the rash
items.title: Patient isolation
items.contentHtml:
Respiratory isolation for 4 days of the rash

  • The patient with measles must maintain respiratory isolation (surgical mask, individual room with negative pressure if possible) from the beginning of the prodrome until 4 days after the appearance of the rash.
  • In immunocompromised patients, isolation is prolonged for the duration of the disease (they can excrete viruses for weeks).
  • Contacts Susceptible individuals must be identified and vaccinated with MMR within 72 hours post-exposure; If this period passes, intravenous immunoglobulin is administered up to 6 days later.
  • The virus is transmitted by respiratory droplets and aerosols; infectivity is high (>90% in non-immune contacts).

items.foco: Differential with rubella and roseola infantum
items.title: Differential diagnosis
items.contentHtml:
Differential with rubella and roseola infantum

  • Rubella: mild prodrome (low-grade fever, headache, suboccipital/postauricular lymphadenopathy), fine non-confluent maculopapular rash, with rapid cephalocaudal progression (disappears in 3 days). There are no Koplik stains. Arthralgia is common in adults.
  • Roseola infantum (exanthema subitum): caused by human herpesvirus 6 and 7. High fever (>39 °C) for 3-5 days that subsides abruptly, followed by a pinkish maculopapular rash on the trunk that extends to the extremities, without catarrhal symptoms or blood spots. Koplik.
  • Measles: prodrome with cough, coryza, conjunctivitis, Koplik's spots, high fever that persists during the rash, confluent maculopapular rash with cephalocaudal progression.
  • Other differentials: scarlet fever (rough punctate rash, pharyngitis, fever, strawberry tongue), Kawasaki disease (prolonged fever, bilateral conjunctivitis, chapped lips, polymorphous rash).
